revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Ishi no Hōden]][[en:Ishi no Hōden]][[fr:Ishi no Hōden]][[it:Ishi-no-Hōden]][[ja:石の宝殿]][[pl:Ishi-no-Hōden]][[ru:Иси но Ходэн]] {{Short description|Megalithic shrine in Takasago, Kansai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ishi no Hōden | native_name =石の宝殿 | native_name_lang = | image = Ishi-no-hoden , 石の宝殿 - panoramio (1).jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ishi no Hōden | map_type = Japan Hyōgo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ishi no Hōden | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|46|57.15|N|134|47|42.78|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|en|Takasago, Hyōgo|qid=Q869037|lt=Takasago, Hyōgo|1=Takasago, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = megalith | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|46|57.15|N|134|47|42.78|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ishi no Hōden'''|石の宝殿||}} is a megalithic monument located in the grounds of the {{nihongo|Ōshiko Jinja|生石神社||}}, a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|en|Takasago, Hyōgo|qid=Q869037|lt=Takasago|1=Takasago, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Of unknown age, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1979 collectively with the nearby {{nihongo|'''Tatsuyama Stone Quarries'''|竜山石採石遺跡|Tatsuyama Ishisaiseki iseki}}, which dates from the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210377|title=石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is also called the {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ukiishi'''|天の浮石||}}.
==Ishi no Hōden== The Ishi-no-Hōden is made from {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|qid=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} and is surrounded on three sides by unprocessed bedrock. With a weight estimated at 500 tons, it measures 6.4 meters wide by 5.7 meters high by 7.2 meters in thickness. In shape, it is carved in the form of two flat rectangular parallelepipeds oriented vertically and sandwiching a small rectangular parallelepiped. One of the sides has a protrusion shaped like the top of a pyramid. The space between the surrounding bedrock and the megalith is wide enough for one adult to pass through, and it is possible to go around (admission fee is required). The monolith is situated in a large depression, which forms a pond at its base. The monolith is carved with a pillar at the center of its base, which is not visible at eye-level, so the monolith appears to be floating above the pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
From 2005 to 2006, laser three-dimensional measurement was carried out to confirm the shape by the Takasago City Board of Education with the cooperation of the {{ill|Otemae University|en|Otemae University|qid=Q7108520|lt=Otemae University|1=Otemae University}} Research Institute; however, the date the monolith was carved, and by whom and for what purpose remain in complete mystery. In legend, the ''[[kami]]'' [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] came from {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} to {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima|1=Harima Province}} and attempted to build a stone palace overnight. Their efforts were thwarted by a rebellion of the ''kami'' of Harima, and the partially completed palace was overthrown. Nevertheless, Ōkuninushi and Sukunabiko decided to protect the land. The monolith is mentioned in the "Harima Kokudo [[Fudoki]]" which is dated to around 713 to 717, which attributes it to {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|qid=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} per the orders of [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], even though when Prince Shotoku became regent, Mononobe no Moriya had long been dead. During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|qid=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold|1=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} made note of the monument and left three detailed sketches of it in the first volume of his work ''Nippon. Archiv zur Beschreibung von Japan und dessen Neben- und Schutzländern'' from 1832.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The monolith is 1.5 kilometers from [[:en:Hoden Station|Hoden Station]] on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|San'yō Main Line|en|San'yō Main Line|qid=Q1193436|lt=San'yō Main Line|1=San'yō Main Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Tatsuyama Stone Quarries== "Tatsuyama stone" is the name given to {{ill|rhyolite|en|rhyolite|qid=Q190727|lt=rhyolite|1=Rhyolite}} welded {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|qid=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} found on the right bank of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Kako River|en|Kako River|qid=Q6349559|lt=Kakogawa River|1=Kako River}}. It is a thick deposit of {{ill|Pyroclastic flow|en|Pyroclastic flow|qid=Q221616|lt=Pyroclastic flow|1=Pyroclastic flow}} ejected by volcanic activity in the late {{ill|Cretaceous|en|Cretaceous|qid=Q44626|lt=Cretaceous|1=Cretaceous}} about 100 million years ago and has the density and material strength suitable as a building material. Traces of stone quarries have been found at numerous locations within the borders of the city of Takasago, of which 162 locations covering 670,000 square meters remain. These quarries have been in operation for over 1700 years, from the Kofun period to then present day, and 31 locations in the Tatsuyama and Hodenyama neighborhoods covering 110,000 squad meters were selected for protection as a National Historic Site in 1979.<ref name="Bunka1"/>
Stone from these quarries was transported by boat from the Hokkesantani River that flows near the quarry, and was used for buildings and stone Buddhas. It was used as the material for many sarcophagus from the middleand late Kofun period and foundation stones for [[:en:Heijo Palace|Heijo Palace]] and various temples in the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato region|1=Yamato Province}}. In more modern times, it was used in the stone walls of [[Himeji Castle]], the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]] and {{ill|National Diet Building|en|National Diet Building|qid=Q1338856|lt=National Diet Building|1=National Diet Building}} and many other buildings.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Isinohôden (Siebold Nippon II Tab. XXIII).jpg|"Isinohôden" in {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|qid=Q77140|lt=Siebold|1=Philipp Franz von Siebold}}'s ''Nippon'' Atsumori & Isinohôden (Siebold Nippon II Tab. XXIV).jpg|Gravestone of {{ill|Taira no Atsumori|en|Taira no Atsumori|qid=Q2284163|lt=Atsumori|1=Taira no Atsumori}} and Isinohôden in {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|qid=Q77140|lt=Siebold|1=Philipp Franz von Siebold}}'s ''Nippon'' Tatsuyama stone.jpg|Tatsuyama stone quarries </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|qid=Q104629006|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)}}
